source.title: Complementary learning systems within the hippocampus: a neural network modelling approach to reconciling episodic memory with statistical learning
source.venue: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences

confidence_rationale: Load-bearing R2 paper. Computational model directly grounded in known hippocampal anatomy (entorhinal -> CA1 monosynaptic; entorhinal -> DG/CA3 -> CA1 trisynaptic) demonstrating that a single anatomical structure can run two distinct learning regimes simultaneously: episode-specific pattern separation (trisynaptic) and across-episode statistical regularity extraction (monosynaptic). This is the most direct mechanistic anchor in the literature for the claim that bottom-up rule discovery has its own substrate distinct from top-down rule application. Phil Trans B venue + extensive subsequent empirical replication (Schapiro 2016 Neuropsychologia, Davachi-lab fMRI work) make it a near-canonical reference.
failure_signatures: An REE substrate that maps all hippocampal-style memory onto a single pattern-separation pathway will fail to reproduce the dissociation between episode memory and statistical regularity learning that Schapiro 2017 demonstrates -- specifically, fast acquisition of regularities under conditions where individual episode separability degrades.

If ARC-064 is collapsed into a single mechanism on top of the existing E1 prior pathway, the model should still recover the across-episode regularity extraction signature; if not, ARC-064 needs a separate write-pathway analog of the monosynaptic CA1 route.

If the bottom-up regularity-extraction pathway is REDUNDANT with sleep-replay consolidation (MECH-272/MECH-285), then disabling sleep replay while leaving online regularity extraction intact should preserve regularity-learning behaviour; if sleep is necessary and the online pathway insufficient, R3 verdict shifts toward offline-dominant.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The hippocampus instantiates both episodic memorisation and statistical regularity extraction through anatomically distinct projections within the same structure: the trisynaptic pathway (EC -> DG -> CA3 -> CA1) supports rapid pattern-separated episodic encoding, while the monosynaptic pathway (EC -> CA1) supports across-episode regularity learning. The two are not functionally interchangeable; CLS theory's classical hippocampus-vs-neocortex tradeoff is partially recapitulated within the hippocampus itself.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-064 (bottom-up rule discovery via behavioural pattern extraction) requires a regularity-extraction pathway that operates on the trajectory record as it is laid down -- analogous to the monosynaptic CA1 route in Schapiro's model. This is architecturally distinct from the existing REE E1 (sensory prediction) and hippocampal episode-encoding (anchor sets, MECH-269) pathways, which are pattern-separated rather than regularity-pooled. The Schapiro mapping suggests ARC-064 should land as a separate write path that pools across episodes by design, not a downstream readout of the existing episode-encoding substrate.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Schapiro et al.'s model is implemented in a connectionist neural-network simulation calibrated to human statistical-learning experiments (visual sequence/community-structure tasks). The mapping to REE's behavioural-trajectory-clustering claim assumes that trajectories are the analog of the temporal-regularity sequences in their stimuli. This analogy is reasonable but not airtight: behavioural patterns in REE involve action selection + state transitions, while their stimuli are passively-observed sensory sequences. Transfer risk: moderate -- the regularity-extraction-vs-episode-separation principle is general, but the specific anatomical division of labour they propose may or may not have a direct analog in REE's substrate.
mapping.source_context: Phil Trans B themed issue on statistical learning. Connectionist neural-network simulation of hippocampal subfield architecture, calibrated against human behavioural and fMRI data on statistical learning (Schapiro lab Princeton, with Norman, Turk-Browne, and Botvinick at DeepMind).

confidence_components.notes: Phil Trans B is high-quality with peer review; Schapiro is the established lead author for the hippocampal-statistical-learning field. Mapping fidelity is high for the architectural claim (two pathways, one anatomical substrate) but lower for whether the specific monosynaptic-vs-trisynaptic division maps cleanly onto REE substrates. Transfer risk moderate because passive-sequence regularity learning -> active-trajectory regularity extraction is a non-trivial domain shift.
